steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage hypothesizes that helicopter surveying leads to overestimates of cougar population density. The blank must complete an example that supports this claim—meaning it should identify a study that used helicopter surveying and reported an anomalously high density. The data table is needed to find that study.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘Juan I. Zanón-Martinez et al. required a study area of 1,179 square kilometers, while the study by David M. Choate et al. required a study area of 1,225 square kilometers.’: compares the geographic area of two studies, not density values. Study area size does not illustrate an overestimate. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘David M. Choate et al. reported a maximum density of 10.24 individuals per 100 square kilometers, higher than that reported by several other studies.’: explicitly states that Choate et al. reported a density higher than others—exactly the kind of anomalously high result that would exemplify an overestimate caused by helicopter surveying. ✓
  • Choice C — ‘Randy D. Johnson reported a maximum density of only 2.78 individuals per 100 square kilometers, lower than that reported by several other studies.’: a low density would illustrate an underestimate, which contradicts the hypothesis. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘Gregory A. Davidson et al. reported a maximum density of 5.50 individuals per 100 square kilometers, while the study by Juan I. Zanón-Martinez et al., which used regular camera trapping, reported a lower maximum density.’: compares two studies without linking either to helicopter surveying or to an unusually high density. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
